title: Jobsonן¿½s Amen
content:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Blessed be the English and all their ways and works.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Cursed be the Infidels, Hereticks, and Turks!ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Amen,ן¿½ quoן¿½ Jobson, ן¿½but where I used to lie
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Was neither Candle, Bell nor Book to curse my brethren by,

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½But a palm-tree in full bearing, bowing down, bowing down,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To a surf that drove unsparing at the brown, walled town,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Conches in a temple, oil-lamps in a dome,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And a low moon out of Africa said: ן¿½This way home!ן¿½ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Blessed be the English and all that they profess.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Cursed be the Savages that prance in nakedness!ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Amen,ן¿½ quoן¿½ Jobson, ן¿½but where I used to lie
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Was neither shirt nor pantaloons to catch my brethren by:

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½But a well-wheel slowly creaking, going round, going round,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ By a water-channel leaking over drowned, warm ground,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Parrots very busy in the trellised pepper-vine,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And a high sun over Asia shouting: ן¿½Rise and shine!ן¿½ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Blessן¿½d be the English and everything they own.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Cursed be the Infidels that bow to wood and stone!ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Amen,ן¿½ quoן¿½ Jobson, ן¿½but where I used to lie
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Was neither pew nor Gospelleer to save my brethren by:

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½But a desert stretched and stricken, left and right, left and right,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Where the piled mirages thicken under white-hot light,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A skull beneath a sand-hill and a viper coiled inside,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And a red wind out of Libya roaring: ן¿½Run and hide!ן¿½ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Blessן¿½d be the English and all they make or do.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Cursן¿½d be the Hereticks who doubt that this is true!ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Amen,ן¿½ quoן¿½ Jobson, ן¿½but where I mean to die
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Is neither rule nor calliper to judge the matter by:

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½But Himalaya heavenward-heading, sheer and vast, sheer and vast,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In a million summits bedding on the last worldן¿½s past,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A certain sacred mountain where the scented cedars climb,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And, the feet of my Beloved hurrying back through Time!ן¿½
meaning: About: Joseph Rudyard Kipling (December 30, 1865 ן¿½ January 18, 1936) was an English author and poet, born in Bombay, India, and best known for his works The Jungle Book (1894), The Second Jungle Book (1895), Just So Stories (1902), and Puck of Pook's Hill (1906); his novel, Kim (1901); his poems, including Mandalay (1890), Gunga Din (1890), and "Ifן¿½" (1910); and his many short stories, including "The Man Who Would Be King" (1888) and the collections Life's Handicap (1891), The Day's Work (1898), and Plain Tales from the Hills (1888). He is regarded as a major "innovator in the art of the short story"; his children's books are enduring classics of children's literature; and his best work speaks to a versatile and luminous narrative gift.
